nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving rivers, streams, and flooded forest areas with soft, slightly acidic water; typically over silty or sandy substrate with leaf litter and submerged vegetation

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (essential for barbel health)
dense planting with open swimming corridors

diet.notes: Accepts a wide range of sinking foods: quality sinking pellets, Corydoras/catfish wafers, and bottom-feeder tablets as a staple. Benefits greatly from regular supplementation with frozen or live bloodworms, Daphnia, Tubifex, and brine shrimp. Scavenges leftover food from the substrate but should not be relied upon solely as a 'tank cleaner' — dedicated sinking feedings are required.

goodWith: small to medium tetras (neon, cardinal, rummy-nose)
peaceful dwarf cichlids (German Blue Ram, Apistogramma)
avoidWith: large or aggressive cichlids (Oscars, Jack Dempseys)
overly boisterous species that outcompete at feeding time

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Brochis splendens; verify current availability and license before use.

summary: A larger-than-average Corydoras relative with brilliant iridescent green-blue flanks — 'splendens' means splendid. Synonymous with Corydoras splendens in trade and literature. Distinguished from typical corys by a higher dorsal-fin ray count and larger adult size (up to 8 cm). Peaceful, hardy, and strongly schooling; an excellent beginner catfish.
careNotes:
The Emerald Green Cory (Brochis splendens) is often sold as 'Emerald Cory' or 'Corydoras splendens' in the trade — these names refer to the same fish. The species was originally described as Callichthys splendens by Castelnau in 1855, later reclassified as Corydoras splendens, and placed in the genus Brochis by Cope in 1872; current ichthyological consensus accepts Brochis splendens as the valid name, though Corydoras splendens remains widespread in hobby literature and retail labelling. NOTE: See also fw-fish-175 ('Emerald Cory', Corydoras splendens) — that entry represents the same species from the Corydoras-synonymy perspective; the two entries are pending merge in end-of-slice cleanup.

This species is notably larger than most Corydoras, reaching 7–8 cm in adults compared to the typical 4–6 cm of common cory species. The brilliant metallic green-blue iridescence of the flanks is its defining visual feature; under aquarium lighting, the body glows a vivid emerald. The higher dorsal-fin ray count (10–12 soft rays vs. 6–8 in most Corydoras) is the morphological characteristic historically used to segregate Brochis from Corydoras.

Fine sand substrate is strongly recommended; gravel can damage the sensitive barbels, leading to bacterial infections and feeding difficulties. A group of at least 6 is required for natural schooling behaviour — underpopulated groups may become shy and hide constantly. The Emerald Green Cory is a facultative air-breather; occasional trips to the surface to gulp air are normal. Copper-based medications are harmful to all callichthyid catfish and must be avoided; use alternative treatments. Weekly partial water changes of 25–30% are strongly advised to maintain low nitrates.
breedingNotes: Moderately achievable in captivity. Breeding is typically triggered by simulating seasonal water changes: cool, slightly acidic water changes (5–8°C below tank temperature) combined with increased live/frozen food feeding mimics the onset of the rainy season. The fish will scatter adhesive eggs on glass, plant leaves, and flat surfaces; eggs should be removed to a separate hatching tank to prevent predation. Fry hatch in 3–5 days and initially require infusoria or commercially prepared fry foods, progressing to micro-worm and baby brine shrimp. Sexual dimorphism is subtle; females are fuller-bodied when gravid and slightly wider when viewed from above. Females are generally larger than males at maturity.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Brochis splendens
primary.notes: Primary species profile. Provided adult size (7.0 cm SL), temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 2–25°H, native range (upper Amazon basin: Peru, Ecuador, Brazil), habitat, taxonomy, diet, and breeding information. Also documents synonymy with Corydoras splendens and Callichthys splendens (Castelnau, 1855).

additional.name: FishBase — Brochis splendens
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Callichthyidae / Siluriformes), max size (8.0 cm TL), temperature range (22–28°C), pH (6.0–8.0), native range (Amazon basin tributaries in Peru, Ecuador, and Brazil). Lists synonyms including Corydoras splendens (Castelnau 1855) and Callichthys splendens. Confirms Brochis splendens as the currently accepted valid name.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Cory Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General corydoras/brochis care guide; confirmed group size recommendation (6+), fine sand substrate requirement, sinking food approach, temperature range 72–82°F (22–28°C), and beginner-level care rating. The Emerald Cory is listed as one of the popular species recommended for community tanks.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Corydoras and Brochis species guide
additional.notes: Documented the higher dorsal-fin ray count (10–12) as the key morphological distinction between Brochis and Corydoras genera. Confirmed larger adult size of B. splendens compared to typical Corydoras and noted the species' strong schooling drive. Also documented the ongoing taxonomic discussion around merging Brochis back into Corydoras — current accepted name remains Brochis splendens per ICZN precedent.

additional.name: Catalog of Fishes (Eschmeyer) — Brochis splendens synonymy
additional.notes: Authoritative nomenclatural reference. Confirms type description as Callichthys splendens Castelnau 1855; subsequent placement in Brochis by Cope 1872; Corydoras splendens as a widely used but junior synonym. Brochis splendens (Cope, 1872) is the currently accepted combination per ichthyological consensus, though some molecular phylogenies suggest Brochis should be synonymised into Corydoras.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; spawning triggered by cool water changes and increased live/frozen food feeding. Adhesive eggs laid on glass and plants; separate fry for best survival. Fry require infusoria as first food.
